vm_hemoglobin.title: Hemoglobin
vm_hemoglobin.content: Hemoglobin (also spelled Haemoglobin) is the iron-containing protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body. Normal levels are roughly 13.5-17.5 g/dL for men and 12.0-15.5 g/dL for women. Low hemoglobin (haemoglobin) causes anemia, leading to fatigue, weakness, pale skin, and shortness of breath. Producing hemoglobin requires adequate iron, vitamin B12, and folate. Foods that help raise hemoglobin: iron-rich foods (spinach, lentils, red meat, beans), vitamin C (improves iron absorption), vitamin B12 and folate sources (eggs, dairy, leafy greens).
vm_hemoglobin.keywords: Hemoglobin, Haemoglobin, HGB, anemia, iron, oxygen, red blood cells, blood test

dis_ckd.title: Chronic Kidney Disease
dis_ckd.content: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) is the gradual loss of kidney function over time, most commonly caused by diabetes and high blood pressure. Symptoms often appear late and include fatigue, swelling in the legs/ankles, changes in urination, and nausea. CKD is staged 1-5 based on estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFR), with stage 5 being kidney failure. Dietary management for CKD typically involves limiting sodium, potassium, and phosphorus intake, moderating protein intake, and staying within fluid limits as advised by a doctor. Regular blood tests (creatinine, BUN, GFR) are used to monitor kidney health.
dis_ckd.keywords: Chronic Kidney Disease, CKD, kidney failure, renal, GFR, creatinine, dialysis, diabetes, blood pressure

dis_vitamin_deficiency_overview.title: Vitamin Deficiency Overview
dis_vitamin_deficiency_overview.content: Vitamin deficiency occurs when the body doesn't get or absorb enough of a specific vitamin, leading to health problems that range from mild to severe. Common deficiency diseases include Scurvy (vitamin C), Rickets/Osteomalacia (vitamin D), Night Blindness (vitamin A), and Beriberi (vitamin B1). Anemia can result from deficiencies in iron, vitamin B12, or folate. Common causes include poor diet, malabsorption disorders, restrictive diets, and certain medications. General prevention includes eating a varied, balanced diet rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and adequate protein, with supplementation recommended by a doctor when a specific deficiency is diagnosed via blood tests.
dis_vitamin_deficiency_overview.keywords: Vitamin deficiency, malnutrition, Scurvy, Rickets, Night Blindness, Beriberi, Anemia, deficiency disease

ill_covid19.title: COVID-19
ill_covid19.content: COVID-19 is a respiratory illness. Source: caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus, spread mainly through respiratory droplets and close contact with an infected person. Symptoms: fever, cough, fatigue, loss of taste or smell, sore throat, and shortness of breath in severe cases. Prevention: vaccination, wearing masks in crowded indoor spaces, good hand hygiene, and staying home when sick. Nutrition support: adequate protein for tissue repair, vitamin C and zinc for immune support, vitamin D, and staying well hydrated. Rest and medical care are essential; nutrition supports recovery but does not replace treatment.
ill_covid19.keywords: COVID-19, COVID, coronavirus disease, SARS-CoV-2, respiratory illness, pandemic

ill_coronavirus.title: Coronavirus
ill_coronavirus.content: Coronaviruses are a family of viruses. Source: spread mainly via respiratory droplets and close contact; the family includes viruses causing the common cold as well as more severe diseases like SARS, MERS, and COVID-19 (caused by SARS-CoV-2). Symptoms: range from mild cold-like symptoms to severe respiratory illness depending on the specific virus. Prevention: hand hygiene, respiratory etiquette (covering coughs/sneezes), avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and vaccination where available. Nutrition support: adequate protein, vitamin C, vitamin D, and zinc, along with good hydration and rest, helps the body recover from viral respiratory infections.
ill_coronavirus.keywords: Coronavirus, virus family, SARS, MERS, respiratory virus

ill_malaria.title: Malaria
ill_malaria.content: Malaria is a parasitic disease. Source: caused by Plasmodium parasites, transmitted through the bite of infected Anopheles mosquitoes (does not spread person-to-person through casual contact). Symptoms: high fever, chills, sweating, headache, nausea, and muscle pain, often occurring in cycles; can cause anemia due to destruction of red blood cells. Prevention: sleeping under insecticide-treated mosquito nets, using mosquito repellent, eliminating standing water where mosquitoes breed, and antimalarial preventive medication for travelers to high-risk areas. Nutrition support: iron and folate-rich foods to help rebuild red blood cells, plenty of fluids during fever, and easily digestible, calorie-dense foods during recovery. Antimalarial medical treatment is essential.
ill_malaria.keywords: Malaria, Plasmodium, mosquito-borne disease, fever, anemia

ill_dengue.title: Dengue Fever
ill_dengue.content: Dengue fever is a viral infection. Source: spread by the bite of infected Aedes mosquitoes, which typically bite during the day and breed in stagnant water. Symptoms: sudden high fever, severe headache, pain behind the eyes, joint and muscle pain, rash, and a drop in platelet count; severe cases can lead to dengue hemorrhagic fever. Prevention: eliminating stagnant water around homes, using mosquito repellent and nets, and wearing long sleeves/pants in high-risk areas. Nutrition support: high fluid intake to prevent dehydration, vitamin C rich foods, and protein-rich foods to support platelet and tissue recovery; papaya leaf extract is a widely used traditional remedy, though medical monitoring of platelet counts is essential.
ill_dengue.keywords: Dengue, dengue fever, Aedes mosquito, platelet count, viral fever

ill_chikungunya.title: Chikungunya
ill_chikungunya.content: Chikungunya is a viral infection closely related to dengue. Source: spread by the bite of infected Aedes mosquitoes. Symptoms: sudden fever and severe joint pain (often the most prominent symptom), muscle pain, headache, and rash; joint pain can persist for weeks or months after the fever resolves. Prevention: eliminating mosquito breeding sites (stagnant water), using mosquito repellent and nets, and wearing protective clothing. Nutrition support: anti-inflammatory foods (omega-3s, turmeric), adequate hydration, and rest during the acute fever phase.
ill_chikungunya.keywords: Chikungunya, chickengunia, chikungunya fever, joint pain, Aedes mosquito

ill_asthma.title: Asthma
ill_asthma.content: Asthma is a chronic respiratory condition. Source: airways become inflamed and narrowed, triggered by allergens, cold air, exercise, respiratory infections, air pollution, or genetic predisposition (it is not contagious). Symptoms: wheezing, shortness of breath, chest tightness, and coughing. Prevention: identifying and avoiding personal triggers, using prescribed control medication consistently, getting flu/pneumonia vaccinations, and keeping indoor air clean. Nutrition support: an anti-inflammatory diet rich in omega-3 fatty acids, fruits, and vegetables; adequate vitamin D; and identifying/avoiding specific food triggers if present. Asthma requires ongoing medical management alongside supportive nutrition.
ill_asthma.keywords: Asthma, wheezing, chronic respiratory disease, airway inflammation

ill_cbc_report.title: CBC Report (Complete Blood Count)
ill_cbc_report.content: A Complete Blood Count (CBC) is a common blood test. It measures red blood cells (RBC), white blood cells (WBC), hemoglobin, hematocrit (PCV), and platelets. Use: screens for and helps diagnose conditions like anemia (low hemoglobin/RBC), infections (high WBC), and clotting disorders (abnormal platelets). Prevention/monitoring: routine CBC testing helps catch nutrient deficiencies and blood disorders early, before symptoms become severe. Understanding your CBC report alongside a doctor's interpretation helps identify nutrient deficiencies (like iron or B12 deficiency) that may need dietary correction.
ill_cbc_report.keywords: CBC, complete blood count, blood test, blood report, RBC, WBC, platelets, hemoglobin

ill_tuberculosis.title: Tuberculosis (TB)
ill_tuberculosis.content: Tuberculosis (TB) is a bacterial infection. Source: caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, primarily affecting the lungs, spread through airborne droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Symptoms: persistent cough (sometimes with blood), unexplained weight loss, night sweats, fever, and fatigue. Prevention: BCG vaccination (given in many countries at birth), good ventilation, early detection and treatment of active cases, and completing the full antibiotic course to prevent drug-resistant strains. Nutrition support: TB commonly causes malnutrition and significant weight loss, so a high-protein, high-calorie diet, along with adequate vitamin D and vitamin A, supports immune function and tissue repair during the long antibiotic treatment course.
ill_tuberculosis.keywords: Tuberculosis, TB, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, lung infection, persistent cough

ill_chickenpox.title: Chickenpox
ill_chickenpox.content: Chickenpox is a highly contagious viral infection. Source: caused by the varicella-zoster virus, spread through direct contact with the rash or through respiratory droplets; most common in children. Symptoms: itchy, blister-like rash, fever, tiredness, and loss of appetite. Prevention: varicella vaccination is highly effective; avoiding contact with infected individuals if not immune. Nutrition support: soft, easy-to-swallow foods if there are mouth sores, plenty of fluids to prevent dehydration, and vitamin C rich foods to support healing; avoid scratching to prevent scarring and secondary infection.
ill_chickenpox.keywords: Chickenpox, chicken pox, varicella-zoster, viral rash

ill_jaundice.title: Jaundice
ill_jaundice.content: Jaundice is a yellowing of the skin and eyes caused by a buildup of bilirubin in the blood. Source: often signals an underlying liver problem (such as hepatitis), bile duct blockage, or excessive red blood cell breakdown, rather than being a disease itself. Prevention: vaccination against hepatitis, avoiding excess alcohol, maintaining a healthy weight to prevent fatty liver disease, and prompt treatment of underlying conditions. Nutrition support: a liver-friendly diet that's low in fat and avoids alcohol, adequate hydration, and easily digestible, high-fiber foods; the underlying cause needs medical diagnosis and treatment.
ill_jaundice.keywords: Jaundice, yellowing skin, bilirubin, liver problem

ill_food_poisoning.title: Food Poisoning
ill_food_poisoning.content: Food poisoning is an illness caused by consuming contaminated food or water. Source: bacteria (like Salmonella, E. coli), viruses, parasites, or their toxins in improperly handled, stored, or cooked food. Symptoms: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, stomach cramps, and sometimes fever. Prevention: thorough handwashing, cooking food to safe temperatures, avoiding cross-contamination, and proper refrigeration of perishables. Nutrition support: oral rehydration solution (ORS) or electrolyte fluids to prevent dehydration, a bland diet (bananas, rice, applesauce, toast) as symptoms ease, and probiotics to help restore healthy gut bacteria; seek medical care if symptoms are severe or persistent.
ill_food_poisoning.keywords: Food poisoning, foodborne illness, contaminated food, gastroenteritis

ill_hepatitis.title: Hepatitis
ill_hepatitis.content: Hepatitis is inflammation of the liver. Source: most often viral infections (Hepatitis A and E spread via contaminated food/water; Hepatitis B, C, and D spread via blood or bodily fluids), though it can also result from alcohol use, toxins, or autoimmune conditions. Symptoms: jaundice, fatigue, abdominal pain, nausea, and dark urine. Prevention: vaccination (available for Hepatitis A and B), safe food/water practices, avoiding sharing needles, and safe sex practices. Nutrition support: a balanced diet with adequate protein for liver repair, avoiding alcohol and fatty/fried foods, staying hydrated, and small frequent meals if appetite is reduced.
ill_hepatitis.keywords: Hepatitis, hepatitis A, hepatitis B, hepatitis C, liver inflammation, viral hepatitis

ill_cholera.title: Cholera
ill_cholera.content: Cholera is a bacterial infection. Source: caused by Vibrio cholerae, usually spread through contaminated drinking water or food, especially where sanitation is poor. Symptoms: severe watery diarrhea and vomiting, which can lead to rapid, life-threatening dehydration. Prevention: access to clean drinking water, proper sanitation, food safety practices, and oral cholera vaccines in high-risk areas. Nutrition support: immediate oral rehydration solution (ORS) with the correct balance of salts and sugar is the most critical intervention, along with zinc supplementation, which has been shown to reduce the duration and severity of diarrhea.
ill_cholera.keywords: Cholera, Vibrio cholerae, severe diarrhea, dehydration, waterborne disease

ill_diarrhea.title: Diarrhea
ill_diarrhea.content: Diarrhea is frequent, loose, or watery stools. Source: commonly caused by infections (viral, bacterial, or parasitic), food intolerances, contaminated water/food, or medications. Symptoms: can lead to dehydration and loss of electrolytes if prolonged. Prevention: safe drinking water, good hand hygiene, proper food handling, and avoiding known personal food triggers. Nutrition support: oral rehydration solution (ORS) or electrolyte drinks, the BRAT diet (bananas, rice, applesauce, toast), probiotics to restore gut bacteria, and temporarily avoiding dairy, fatty, and spicy foods until symptoms resolve.
ill_diarrhea.keywords: Diarrhea, diarrhoea, loose stools, dehydration, gastrointestinal illness

ill_depression.title: Depression
ill_depression.content: Depression is a common mental health condition causing persistent sadness, loss of interest in activities, and difficulty with daily functioning. Source: arises from a combination of genetic, biological, environmental, and psychological factors (e.g. chronic stress, major life changes, trauma); it requires proper diagnosis and treatment by a mental health professional (therapy and/or medication). Prevention: maintaining social connections, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, stress management, and seeking help early when symptoms appear. Nutrition support: diets rich in omega-3 fatty acids, B vitamins, vitamin D, and probiotics (supporting the gut-brain axis) are associated with better mood regulation, but nutrition is a complement to professional treatment, not a replacement for it. If you or someone you know is struggling, please reach out to a healthcare provider.
ill_depression.keywords: Depression, mental health, mood disorder, gut-brain axis

ill_typhoid.title: Typhoid Fever
ill_typhoid.content: Typhoid fever is a bacterial infection. Source: caused by Salmonella Typhi, spread through food or water contaminated with the feces of an infected person. Symptoms: prolonged high fever, weakness, stomach pain, headache, and loss of appetite. Prevention: typhoid vaccination, safe drinking water, good hand hygiene, and proper sanitation. Nutrition support: a soft, high-calorie, easily digestible diet during recovery (khichdi-style meals, soups, bananas), adequate fluids, and small frequent meals since appetite is often reduced; antibiotic treatment is essential alongside nutritional support.
ill_typhoid.keywords: Typhoid, typhoid fever, Salmonella Typhi, prolonged fever

ill_flu.title: Flu (Influenza)
ill_flu.content: The flu (influenza) is a contagious viral respiratory infection. Source: spreads mainly through respiratory droplets when infected people cough, sneeze, or talk. Symptoms: fever, chills, muscle aches, cough, sore throat, congestion, and fatigue. Prevention: annual flu vaccination is the best prevention, along with good hand hygiene and avoiding close contact with sick individuals. Nutrition support: vitamin C and zinc rich foods to support immune function, warm fluids and soups (chicken soup has real anti-inflammatory and hydrating benefits), and adequate rest.
ill_flu.keywords: Flu, influenza, viral respiratory infection, seasonal flu

ill_hiv.title: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus)
ill_hiv.content: HIV is a virus that attacks the body's immune system, specifically CD4 (T-helper) cells. Source: spread through contact with certain body fluids of an infected person - unprotected sexual contact, sharing needles/syringes, mother-to-child transmission during pregnancy/birth/breastfeeding, or contaminated blood transfusions; it does not spread through casual contact, saliva, or air. Symptoms: flu-like illness within 2-4 weeks of infection (fever, fatigue, swollen lymph nodes), followed by a long asymptomatic period; without treatment, it progressively weakens the immune system. Prevention: consistent condom use, not sharing needles, pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) medication for high-risk individuals, regular testing, and antiretroviral treatment during pregnancy to prevent mother-to-child transmission. Nutrition support: a high-protein, adequate-calorie diet to maintain weight and muscle mass, micronutrient support (vitamin A, C, E, zinc, selenium) for immune function, and extra food safety care due to increased infection vulnerability.
ill_hiv.keywords: HIV, human immunodeficiency virus, CD4 cells, immune system virus

ill_aids.title: AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome)
ill_aids.content: AIDS is the most advanced stage of HIV infection. Source: occurs when HIV has severely damaged the immune system (very low CD4 cell count) if left untreated, leaving the body vulnerable to opportunistic infections and certain cancers; the underlying cause is the same as HIV transmission (unprotected sex, needle sharing, mother-to-child transmission). Symptoms: rapid weight loss, recurring fever, prolonged swollen lymph glands, chronic diarrhea, persistent sores, pneumonia, and memory problems, largely from opportunistic infections the weakened immune system can no longer fight off. Prevention: the same prevention measures as HIV, plus - most importantly - early HIV diagnosis and consistent antiretroviral therapy (ART), which prevents progression from HIV to AIDS almost entirely. Nutrition support: high-protein, high-calorie diet with micronutrient support (vitamin A, C, E, zinc, selenium), strict food safety (avoiding raw/undercooked foods) due to severely weakened immunity, and small frequent meals if appetite is poor.
ill_aids.keywords: AIDS, acquired immunodeficiency syndrome, advanced HIV, opportunistic infections

ill_cancer.title: Cancer
ill_cancer.content: Cancer is a group of diseases involving abnormal, uncontrolled cell growth that can invade nearby tissue and spread to other parts of the body. Source: caused by genetic mutations in cells, driven by risk factors including tobacco use, excessive alcohol, poor diet, obesity, physical inactivity, UV/radiation exposure, certain infections (e.g. HPV, Hepatitis B/C), family history, and environmental carcinogens. Symptoms: vary widely by type, but general warning signs include unexplained weight loss, persistent fatigue, unusual lumps, changes in skin, a persistent cough, and changes in bowel/bladder habits. Prevention: avoiding tobacco, limiting alcohol, maintaining a healthy weight, regular physical activity, a balanced diet rich in fruits/vegetables/fiber, limiting processed and red meat, protecting skin from UV exposure, HPV and Hepatitis B vaccination, and regular screenings for early detection. Nutrition support: antioxidant-rich fruits and vegetables, adequate protein (especially important during treatment), fiber for digestive/colon health, and limiting processed meats and excess alcohol; nutrition can support prevention and recovery but medical treatment is essential.
ill_cancer.keywords: Cancer, tumor, malignant cells, oncology, carcinogen

canc_breast.title: Breast Cancer
canc_breast.content: Breast cancer is a cancer that forms in the cells of the breast, most commonly in the ducts or lobules. Source: risk factors include age, family history/genetic mutations (BRCA1/BRCA2), early menstruation/late menopause, obesity, alcohol use, and hormone therapy. Symptoms: a new lump or mass, breast swelling, skin dimpling, nipple discharge or inversion, and breast or nipple pain. Prevention: regular self-exams and mammogram screening for early detection, maintaining a healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular physical activity, and breastfeeding when possible. Nutrition support: a diet rich in fruits, vegetables, and fiber, limiting processed and red meat, and maintaining a healthy weight all help lower risk and support recovery during treatment.
canc_breast.keywords: Breast cancer, mammogram, BRCA, breast lump

canc_lung.title: Lung Cancer
canc_lung.content: Lung cancer is a cancer that begins in the cells of the lungs. Source: the leading cause is tobacco smoking (including secondhand smoke), followed by exposure to radon gas, asbestos, air pollution, and family history. Symptoms: a persistent cough, coughing up blood, chest pain, hoarseness, unexplained weight loss, and shortness of breath. Prevention: not smoking (or quitting), avoiding secondhand smoke, testing homes for radon, and avoiding occupational carcinogen exposure. Nutrition support: a diet rich in fruits and vegetables (particularly ones with antioxidants) is associated with lower risk; adequate protein and calories are important to prevent weight loss during treatment.
canc_lung.keywords: Lung cancer, smoking, radon, persistent cough

canc_skin.title: Skin Cancer (Melanoma)
canc_skin.content: Skin cancer is abnormal growth of skin cells, most often on skin exposed to the sun; melanoma is the most serious type. Source: primarily caused by ultraviolet (UV) radiation from sun exposure or tanning beds; fair skin, many moles, and family history increase risk. Symptoms: a new mole or a change in an existing mole's size, shape, or color; a sore that doesn't heal; or a spot that looks different from others. Prevention: using sunscreen, wearing protective clothing, avoiding peak sun hours and tanning beds, and regular skin self-exams for early detection. Nutrition support: antioxidant-rich foods (vitamin C, vitamin E, beta-carotene) may help protect skin cells from UV-related damage, alongside sun-safety practices.
canc_skin.keywords: Skin cancer, melanoma, UV radiation, mole changes

canc_leukemia.title: Blood Cancer (Leukemia)
canc_leukemia.content: Leukemia is a cancer of the blood-forming tissues, including bone marrow, that leads to production of abnormal white blood cells. Source: exact cause is often unknown; risk factors include genetic disorders, previous chemotherapy/radiation, exposure to certain chemicals (like benzene), and family history. Symptoms: fatigue, frequent infections, easy bruising or bleeding, fever, and unexplained weight loss. Prevention: no guaranteed prevention exists since causes are largely genetic/unknown, but avoiding exposure to industrial chemicals and radiation when possible may reduce risk. Nutrition support: a high-protein, nutrient-dense diet supports blood cell recovery during treatment; food safety is especially important since chemotherapy weakens the immune system.
canc_leukemia.keywords: Leukemia, blood cancer, bone marrow, white blood cells

canc_colorectal.title: Colorectal Cancer
canc_colorectal.content: Colorectal cancer is cancer that begins in the colon or rectum, often starting as precancerous polyps. Source: risk factors include age, a diet high in red/processed meat and low in fiber, obesity, physical inactivity, smoking, alcohol, and family history/inflammatory bowel disease. Symptoms: changes in bowel habits, blood in stool, persistent abdominal discomfort, unexplained weight loss, and fatigue. Prevention: regular colonoscopy screening after age 45-50 (or earlier with family history) to detect and remove polyps before they become cancerous, along with a high-fiber diet and limiting processed/red meat. Nutrition support: high-fiber foods (whole grains, fruits, vegetables, legumes), calcium, and limiting processed and red meat are associated with lower risk.
canc_colorectal.keywords: Colorectal cancer, colon cancer, polyps, colonoscopy

canc_prostate.title: Prostate Cancer
canc_prostate.content: Prostate cancer is cancer that develops in the prostate gland in men. Source: risk increases with age, family history, and is more common in men of African descent; diet and obesity may also play a role. Symptoms: often none in early stages; later symptoms include difficulty urinating, blood in urine or semen, and pelvic discomfort. Prevention: regular PSA blood test and screening discussions with a doctor after age 50 (or earlier with family history), maintaining a healthy weight, and regular physical activity. Nutrition support: a diet rich in vegetables (especially tomatoes/lycopene), limiting red meat and high-fat dairy, and maintaining a healthy weight are associated with lower risk.
canc_prostate.keywords: Prostate cancer, PSA test, men's health

canc_cervical.title: Cervical Cancer
canc_cervical.content: Cervical cancer is cancer that occurs in the cells of the cervix, almost always caused by persistent infection with high-risk types of human papillomavirus (HPV). Source: HPV infection (sexually transmitted) is the primary cause; smoking and a weakened immune system increase risk. Symptoms: often none in early stages; later symptoms include abnormal vaginal bleeding or discharge and pelvic pain. Prevention: HPV vaccination, regular Pap smear/HPV screening for early detection of precancerous changes, and safe sex practices. Nutrition support: a diet rich in fruits and vegetables supports overall immune function, which helps the body clear HPV infections before they progress.
canc_cervical.keywords: Cervical cancer, HPV, Pap smear, women's health

canc_liver.title: Liver Cancer
canc_liver.content: Liver cancer is cancer that begins in the cells of the liver. Source: chronic Hepatitis B or C infection, cirrhosis (often from alcohol use or fatty liver disease), and aflatoxin exposure (a toxin from mold on stored grains/nuts) are major risk factors. Symptoms: unexplained weight loss, loss of appetite, upper abdominal pain, jaundice, and abdominal swelling. Prevention: Hepatitis B vaccination, avoiding excess alcohol, maintaining a healthy weight to prevent fatty liver disease, and proper food storage to avoid aflatoxin contamination. Nutrition support: a balanced, liver-friendly diet low in fat and alcohol, with adequate protein, supports liver health and recovery.

canc_stomach.title: Stomach Cancer
canc_stomach.content: Stomach (gastric) cancer is cancer that forms in the lining of the stomach. Source: chronic H. pylori bacterial infection, a diet high in smoked/salted/pickled foods, smoking, and family history are key risk factors. Symptoms: indigestion, stomach pain, unintended weight loss, feeling full after small meals, and nausea. Prevention: treating H. pylori infection, limiting smoked/salted/processed foods, not smoking, and eating plenty of fresh fruits and vegetables. Nutrition support: a diet rich in fresh fruits and vegetables (particularly citrus and leafy greens) and low in salted/smoked/processed foods is associated with lower risk.
canc_stomach.keywords: Stomach cancer, gastric cancer, H. pylori

canc_oral.title: Oral Cancer
canc_oral.content: Oral cancer is cancer that develops in any part of the mouth, including the lips, tongue, and gums. Source: tobacco use (smoking or chewing) and heavy alcohol use are the leading causes, especially combined; HPV infection is also a growing risk factor. Symptoms: a mouth sore that doesn't heal, a lump or thickening in the cheek, white or red patches, and difficulty chewing or swallowing. Prevention: avoiding tobacco in all forms, limiting alcohol, HPV vaccination, and regular dental checkups for early detection. Nutrition support: a diet rich in fruits and vegetables supports oral tissue health; avoiding tobacco and excess alcohol remains the most important preventive step.
canc_oral.keywords: Oral cancer, mouth cancer, tobacco, HPV

canc_lymphoma.title: Lymphoma
canc_lymphoma.content: Lymphoma is a cancer of the lymphatic system, part of the body's immune system, categorized broadly into Hodgkin and non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Source: exact cause is often unknown; risk factors include a weakened immune system, certain viral infections (like Epstein-Barr virus), and family history. Symptoms: painless swelling of lymph nodes (neck, armpit, groin), fatigue, fever, night sweats, and unexplained weight loss. Prevention: no guaranteed prevention exists, but maintaining a healthy immune system and avoiding known viral risk factors when possible may help. Nutrition support: a nutrient-dense, high-protein diet supports the immune system and helps maintain strength during treatment.
canc_lymphoma.keywords: Lymphoma, Hodgkin lymphoma, lymph nodes, lymphatic system

canc_pancreatic.title: Pancreatic Cancer
canc_pancreatic.content: Pancreatic cancer is cancer that forms in the tissues of the pancreas, an organ involved in digestion and blood sugar regulation. Source: risk factors include smoking, chronic pancreatitis, obesity, type 2 diabetes, and family history. Symptoms: often vague in early stages; later symptoms include abdominal pain radiating to the back, jaundice, unexplained weight loss, and new-onset diabetes. Prevention: not smoking, maintaining a healthy weight, limiting alcohol, and managing chronic pancreatitis or diabetes. Nutrition support: small, frequent, easily digestible meals with adequate protein and calories are important, since the pancreas's digestive role is often affected; a dietitian's guidance is valuable during treatment.

fv_apple.title: Apple
fv_apple.content: Apple is a popular fruit, available in many varieties (red, green, yellow). Key nutrients: fiber (especially pectin), vitamin C, and antioxidants like quercetin, concentrated mostly in the skin. Health benefits: supports heart health and healthy cholesterol levels, aids digestion, and helps regulate blood sugar due to its fiber content. Best eaten with the skin on for maximum fiber and antioxidants.

fv_banana.title: Banana
fv_banana.content: Banana is a widely eaten tropical fruit. Key nutrients: potassium, vitamin B6, vitamin C, and fiber. Health benefits: supports heart and muscle function through potassium, provides quick energy from natural sugars, and aids digestion. A convenient, easily digestible pre- or post-exercise snack.

fv_orange.title: Orange
fv_orange.content: Orange is a citrus fruit known for its high vitamin C content. Key nutrients: vitamin C, fiber, folate, and antioxidants (flavonoids). Health benefits: strengthens immune function, supports collagen production and skin health, and improves iron absorption from plant foods when eaten together.
fv_orange.keywords: Orange, citrus, vitamin c, fruit nutrition

fv_mango.title: Mango
fv_mango.content: Mango is a sweet tropical fruit often called the 'king of fruits'. Key nutrients: vitamin A, vitamin C, folate, and fiber. Health benefits: supports eye health and immune function through vitamin A and C, and aids digestion; best enjoyed in moderation due to its natural sugar content, especially for those managing blood sugar.
fv_mango.keywords: Mango, tropical fruit, vitamin a, fruit nutrition

fv_kiwi.title: Kiwi
fv_kiwi.content: Kiwi (kiwifruit) is a small fruit with green or gold flesh and edible black seeds. Key nutrients: exceptionally high in vitamin C (more per serving than an orange), vitamin K, fiber, and potassium. Health benefits: strong immune support, aids digestion due to fiber and the enzyme actinidin, and supports healthy blood clotting via vitamin K.
fv_kiwi.keywords: Kiwi, kiwifruit, vitamin c, fruit nutrition

fv_grapes.title: Grapes
fv_grapes.content: Grapes are small, sweet fruits that grow in clusters, available in green, red, and black varieties. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, and antioxidants like resveratrol (concentrated in the skin, especially red/black grapes). Health benefits: antioxidants support heart health and may help reduce inflammation; best eaten in moderation due to natural sugar content.

fv_papaya.title: Papaya
fv_papaya.content: Papaya is a tropical fruit with orange flesh and black seeds. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin A, folate, and the digestive enzyme papain. Health benefits: aids digestion (papain helps break down protein), supports immune function and eye health, and has anti-inflammatory properties.
fv_papaya.keywords: Papaya, papain, tropical fruit, fruit nutrition

fv_pineapple.title: Pineapple
fv_pineapple.content: Pineapple is a tropical fruit known for its sweet-tart flavor. Key nutrients: vitamin C, manganese, and the enzyme bromelain. Health benefits: bromelain has anti-inflammatory properties and aids protein digestion; vitamin C supports immune function and wound healing.
fv_pineapple.keywords: Pineapple, bromelain, tropical fruit, fruit nutrition

fv_guava.title: Guava
fv_guava.content: Guava is a tropical fruit with edible skin and seeds. Key nutrients: extremely high in vitamin C (often more than citrus fruits), fiber, and lycopene (in pink-fleshed varieties). Health benefits: strong immune support, aids digestion due to high fiber content, and supports heart health through lycopene and potassium.
fv_guava.keywords: Guava, vitamin c, tropical fruit, fruit nutrition

fv_pomegranate.title: Pomegranate
fv_pomegranate.content: Pomegranate is a fruit with a thick skin containing many juicy, seeded arils. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, fiber, and powerful antioxidants called punicalagins. Health benefits: supports heart health, has strong anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, and may support joint health.

fv_watermelon.title: Watermelon
fv_watermelon.content: Watermelon is a large, water-rich fruit (about 92% water). Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin A, and the antioxidant lycopene. Health benefits: excellent for hydration, supports heart health and skin health through lycopene and vitamin C, and is naturally low in calories.

fv_strawberry.title: Strawberry
fv_strawberry.content: Strawberry is a sweet, red berry. Key nutrients: very high in vitamin C, manganese, folate, and antioxidants. Health benefits: supports immune function and skin health, and antioxidants may help reduce inflammation and support heart health.
fv_strawberry.keywords: Strawberry, berries, vitamin c, fruit nutrition

fv_blueberry.title: Blueberry
fv_blueberry.content: Blueberry is a small, dark blue berry known for its high antioxidant content. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, fiber, and anthocyanins (the antioxidant giving them their color). Health benefits: supports brain health and memory, may reduce inflammation, and supports heart health.

fv_raspberry.title: Raspberry
fv_raspberry.content: Raspberry is a small, red or black aggregate fruit. Key nutrients: high in fiber, vitamin C, manganese, and antioxidants. Health benefits: supports digestive health due to high fiber content, immune support from vitamin C, and antioxidants that may help reduce inflammation.

fv_muskmelon.title: Muskmelon (Cantaloupe)
fv_muskmelon.content: Muskmelon (cantaloupe) is a sweet, orange-fleshed melon. Key nutrients: vitamin A, vitamin C, and potassium. Health benefits: supports eye health and immune function through vitamin A and C, and is hydrating with a high water content.

fv_pear.title: Pear
fv_pear.content: Pear is a sweet fruit with a grainy texture, eaten with or without the skin. Key nutrients: fiber (especially in the skin), vitamin C, and vitamin K. Health benefits: supports digestive health and healthy cholesterol through its high fiber content, and provides steady energy from natural sugars.

fv_plum.title: Plum
fv_plum.content: Plum is a stone fruit with smooth skin, available in many varieties. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, and antioxidants. Health benefits: supports digestive health (dried plums/prunes are well known for this), supports bone health via vitamin K, and antioxidants support overall cell health.
fv_plum.keywords: Plum, prune, stone fruit, fruit nutrition

fv_peach.title: Peach
fv_peach.content: Peach is a sweet, fuzzy-skinned stone fruit. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin A, and fiber. Health benefits: supports skin and immune health through vitamin C, and aids digestion through its fiber content.

fv_apricot.title: Apricot
fv_apricot.content: Apricot is a small, orange stone fruit, eaten fresh or dried. Key nutrients: vitamin A, vitamin C, potassium, and fiber. Health benefits: supports eye health through its high vitamin A (beta-carotene) content, and supports heart health and digestion.
fv_apricot.keywords: Apricot, stone fruit, beta-carotene, fruit nutrition

fv_fig.title: Fig
fv_fig.content: Fig is a soft, sweet fruit with a fiber-rich, seeded interior, eaten fresh or dried. Key nutrients: fiber, calcium, potassium, and antioxidants. Health benefits: supports digestive health and, notably, provides more calcium than many other fruits, supporting bone health.

fv_lemon_lime.title: Lemon and Lime
fv_lemon_lime.content: Lemon and lime are tart citrus fruits, usually used for their juice and zest rather than eaten whole. Key nutrients: very high in vitamin C, along with citric acid and flavonoid antioxidants. Health benefits: supports immune function, improves iron absorption from plant foods when added to meals, and adds flavor to water/dishes without added sugar.
fv_lemon_lime.keywords: Lemon, lime, citrus, vitamin c, fruit nutrition

fv_avocado.title: Avocado
fv_avocado.content: Avocado is a nutrient-dense fruit known for its creamy texture and healthy fat content. Key nutrients: monounsaturated (heart-healthy) fats, fiber, potassium, vitamin E, and folate. Health benefits: supports heart health through healthy fats, aids nutrient absorption of fat-soluble vitamins from other foods, and supports satiety due to its fat and fiber content.
fv_avocado.keywords: Avocado, healthy fats, monounsaturated fat, fruit nutrition

fv_coconut.title: Coconut
fv_coconut.content: Coconut is a fruit whose flesh, water, and oil are all commonly consumed. Key nutrients: coconut flesh is high in fiber and medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs); coconut water is rich in electrolytes like potassium. Health benefits: coconut water supports hydration and electrolyte balance; coconut flesh provides fiber, though it is also high in saturated fat, so moderation is advised.
fv_coconut.keywords: Coconut, coconut water, MCT, fruit nutrition

fv_spinach.title: Spinach
fv_spinach.content: Spinach is a leafy green vegetable. Key nutrients: iron, vitamin K, vitamin A, folate, and antioxidants (lutein). Health benefits: supports healthy blood (iron and folate for red blood cell production), bone health via vitamin K, and eye health via lutein and vitamin A. Pairing with vitamin C rich foods improves iron absorption.
fv_spinach.keywords: Spinach, leafy greens, iron, vegetable nutrition

fv_broccoli.title: Broccoli
fv_broccoli.content: Broccoli is a cruciferous vegetable with edible flower buds and stalk. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, fiber, and sulforaphane (a compound linked to cancer-protective effects). Health benefits: strong immune and bone support, aids digestion, and its sulforaphane content is associated with reduced cancer risk in research.
fv_broccoli.keywords: Broccoli, cruciferous vegetable, sulforaphane, vegetable nutrition

fv_carrot.title: Carrot
fv_carrot.content: Carrot is a root vegetable, typically orange, though also found in purple, yellow, and white varieties. Key nutrients: very high in beta-carotene (converted to vitamin A), fiber, and vitamin K. Health benefits: supports eye health and immune function through vitamin A, and aids digestion through fiber.
fv_carrot.keywords: Carrot, beta-carotene, root vegetable, vegetable nutrition

fv_tomato.title: Tomato
fv_tomato.content: Tomato is botanically a fruit but commonly used as a vegetable. Key nutrients: vitamin C, potassium, folate, and lycopene (an antioxidant more available when tomatoes are cooked). Health benefits: supports heart health through lycopene and potassium, and immune function through vitamin C.

fv_potato.title: Potato
fv_potato.content: Potato is a starchy root vegetable and a staple food in many cuisines. Key nutrients: vitamin C, potassium (more than a banana), vitamin B6, and fiber (especially in the skin). Health benefits: provides energy through carbohydrates, supports heart function via potassium; preparation method (baked/boiled vs. fried) significantly affects its overall healthfulness.
fv_potato.keywords: Potato, starchy vegetable, potassium, vegetable nutrition

fv_onion.title: Onion
fv_onion.content: Onion is a pungent bulb vegetable used widely in cooking. Key nutrients: vitamin C, fiber, and antioxidants (quercetin) along with prebiotic fibers. Health benefits: quercetin has anti-inflammatory properties, and its prebiotic fiber supports healthy gut bacteria.

fv_cucumber.title: Cucumber
fv_cucumber.content: Cucumber is a high-water-content vegetable, often eaten raw. Key nutrients: mostly water (about 95%), with some vitamin K and potassium. Health benefits: excellent for hydration, low in calories, and its fiber (especially the skin) supports digestion.
fv_cucumber.keywords: Cucumber, hydration, low calorie, vegetable nutrition

fv_cauliflower.title: Cauliflower
fv_cauliflower.content: Cauliflower is a cruciferous vegetable, related to broccoli and cabbage. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, fiber, and choline. Health benefits: supports immune and bone health, aids digestion, and is a popular low-carbohydrate substitute for grains like rice in many diets.
fv_cauliflower.keywords: Cauliflower, cruciferous vegetable, low carb, vegetable nutrition

fv_cabbage.title: Cabbage
fv_cabbage.content: Cabbage is a leafy cruciferous vegetable, available in green, red/purple, and savoy varieties. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, and fiber. Health benefits: supports immune and bone health, aids digestion, and fermented cabbage (like sauerkraut/kimchi) also provides probiotics for gut health.
fv_cabbage.keywords: Cabbage, cruciferous vegetable, sauerkraut, vegetable nutrition

fv_bell_pepper.title: Bell Pepper (Capsicum)
fv_bell_pepper.content: Bell pepper (capsicum) is a sweet pepper available in green, red, yellow, and orange varieties. Key nutrients: very high in vitamin C (red peppers have more than oranges), vitamin A, and antioxidants. Health benefits: strong immune support, supports eye health, and adds color/nutrients to meals with few calories.
fv_bell_pepper.keywords: Bell pepper, capsicum, vitamin c, vegetable nutrition

fv_sweet_potato.title: Sweet Potato
fv_sweet_potato.content: Sweet potato is a starchy root vegetable with orange, purple, or white flesh. Key nutrients: very high in beta-carotene (vitamin A), fiber, and potassium. Health benefits: supports eye health and immune function through vitamin A, aids digestion via fiber, and has a lower glycemic impact than regular potatoes when boiled.
fv_sweet_potato.keywords: Sweet potato, beta-carotene, root vegetable, vegetable nutrition

fv_beetroot.title: Beetroot
fv_beetroot.content: Beetroot is a deep red-purple root vegetable. Key nutrients: folate, potassium, fiber, and nitrates (natural compounds that convert to nitric oxide in the body). Health benefits: nitrates may support healthy blood pressure and exercise performance, and folate supports red blood cell production.

recipe_choc_milkshake.title: Classic Chocolate Milkshake
recipe_choc_milkshake.content: Recipe: Classic Chocolate Milkshake. Blend milk, vanilla ice cream, and chocolate syrup until smooth and creamy. Calories: 320. Protein: 8g. Calcium: 280mg (notably rich). A classic indulgent treat; best enjoyed in moderation due to added sugar content.
recipe_choc_milkshake.keywords: Chocolate milkshake, milkshake recipe, dessert drink, chocolate

recipe_strawberry_milkshake.title: Strawberry Milkshake
recipe_strawberry_milkshake.content: Recipe: Strawberry Milkshake. Blend fresh strawberries, milk, and vanilla ice cream until smooth. Calories: 260. Protein: 7g. Vitamin C: 28mg (notably rich). Fresh strawberries add real vitamin C and antioxidants to this classic fruity shake.
recipe_strawberry_milkshake.keywords: Strawberry milkshake, fruit milkshake, dessert drink

recipe_banana_pb_milkshake.title: Banana Peanut Butter Protein Milkshake
recipe_banana_pb_milkshake.content: Recipe: Banana Peanut Butter Protein Milkshake. Blend banana, milk, peanut butter, and a scoop of protein powder until smooth. Calories: 380. Protein: 24g (notably rich). Potassium: 550mg (notably rich). A high-protein, potassium-rich milkshake, great as a post-workout recovery drink or a filling breakfast.
recipe_banana_pb_milkshake.keywords: Banana milkshake, protein milkshake, peanut butter, post workout, high protein

recipe_mango_milkshake.title: Mango Milkshake
recipe_mango_milkshake.content: Recipe: Mango Milkshake. Blend ripe mango, milk, and a little sugar or honey until smooth and creamy. Calories: 250. Protein: 6g. Vitamin A: 45% RDA (notably rich). Vitamin C: 20mg. A tropical, vitamin-A-rich shake that's a popular summer favorite.
recipe_mango_milkshake.keywords: Mango milkshake, fruit milkshake, tropical drink

recipe_vanilla_milkshake.title: Vanilla Milkshake
recipe_vanilla_milkshake.content: Recipe: Vanilla Milkshake. Blend milk, vanilla ice cream, and a splash of vanilla extract until thick and smooth. Calories: 300. Protein: 8g. Calcium: 270mg (notably rich). A simple, classic milkshake base that pairs well with any mix-in (fruit, cocoa, nut butter).
recipe_vanilla_milkshake.keywords: Vanilla milkshake, classic milkshake, dessert drink

recipe_protein_power_milkshake.title: Protein Power Milkshake
recipe_protein_power_milkshake.content: Recipe: Protein Power Milkshake. Blend milk (or plant milk), a scoop of whey or plant protein powder, a banana, and ice until smooth. Calories: 280. Protein: 30g (notably rich). Calcium: 300mg (notably rich). Designed as a high-protein recovery drink for muscle repair after exercise, or a quick high-protein breakfast.
recipe_protein_power_milkshake.keywords: Protein milkshake, muscle recovery, high protein, fitness drink, post workout

recipe_oreo_milkshake.title: Oreo Cookies and Cream Milkshake
recipe_oreo_milkshake.content: Recipe: Oreo Cookies and Cream Milkshake. Blend milk, vanilla ice cream, and crushed Oreo cookies until thick and smooth. Calories: 420. Protein: 8g. An indulgent dessert-style milkshake best enjoyed occasionally as a treat rather than a regular drink.
recipe_oreo_milkshake.keywords: Oreo milkshake, cookies and cream, dessert drink

recipe_mocha_milkshake.title: Mocha Coffee Milkshake
recipe_mocha_milkshake.content: Recipe: Mocha Coffee Milkshake. Blend cold coffee or espresso, milk, chocolate syrup, and vanilla ice cream until smooth. Calories: 310. Protein: 7g. A caffeinated dessert drink combining coffee and chocolate flavors; contains caffeine, so best enjoyed earlier in the day.
recipe_mocha_milkshake.keywords: Mocha milkshake, coffee milkshake, caffeine drink

recipe_mixed_berry_milkshake.title: Mixed Berry Milkshake
recipe_mixed_berry_milkshake.content: Recipe: Mixed Berry Milkshake. Blend a mix of strawberries, blueberries, and raspberries with milk and a little honey until smooth. Calories: 230. Protein: 6g. Vitamin C: 35mg (notably rich). A naturally antioxidant-rich shake from the combined berries, with less added sugar than ice-cream-based shakes.
recipe_mixed_berry_milkshake.keywords: Berry milkshake, mixed berry, antioxidant drink, fruit milkshake

recipe_avocado_banana_milkshake.title: Avocado Banana Milkshake
recipe_avocado_banana_milkshake.content: Recipe: Avocado Banana Milkshake. Blend avocado, banana, milk, and a touch of honey until creamy and smooth. Calories: 340. Protein: 7g. Potassium: 700mg (notably rich). A creamy, nutrient-dense shake rich in healthy monounsaturated fats and potassium, without needing ice cream for its thick texture.
recipe_avocado_banana_milkshake.keywords: Avocado milkshake, banana milkshake, healthy fats, potassium

pf_eggs.title: Eggs
pf_eggs.content: Eggs are a nutrient-dense whole food and one of the most complete protein sources available. Key nutrients: high-quality complete protein (all 9 essential amino acids), vitamin B12, vitamin D, choline, selenium, and riboflavin; the yolk also contains lutein and zeaxanthin, antioxidants that support eye health. Health benefits: supports muscle maintenance and growth, choline supports brain and liver health, and eggs are an affordable, versatile source of high-quality protein. Modern nutrition research shows dietary cholesterol from eggs has a much smaller effect on blood cholesterol for most people than once believed, so moderate egg consumption (about 1 egg/day) fits well within a healthy diet for most adults.
pf_eggs.keywords: Eggs, egg nutrition, complete protein, choline, protein source

recipe_scrambled_eggs.title: Scrambled Eggs
recipe_scrambled_eggs.content: Recipe: Scrambled Eggs. Whisk eggs with a splash of milk, season with salt and pepper, and cook gently over low heat, stirring continuously until soft and creamy. Calories: 180. Protein: 13g (notably rich). A quick, high-protein breakfast staple; add vegetables like spinach or tomato for extra nutrients.
recipe_scrambled_eggs.keywords: Scrambled eggs, egg recipe, breakfast, high protein

recipe_boiled_eggs.title: Boiled Eggs (Hard and Soft-Boiled)
recipe_boiled_eggs.content: Recipe: Boiled Eggs. Place eggs in a pot of water, bring to a boil, then simmer 6-7 minutes for soft-boiled or 10-12 minutes for hard-boiled, then cool in cold water before peeling. Calories: 78 (per egg). Protein: 6g (notably rich). One of the simplest, most portable high-protein snacks, with no added fat needed.
recipe_boiled_eggs.keywords: Boiled eggs, hard boiled egg, soft boiled egg, egg recipe, snack

recipe_veg_omelette.title: Vegetable Omelette
recipe_veg_omelette.content: Recipe: Vegetable Omelette. Whisk eggs, pour into a hot pan, and fill with sauteed onions, tomatoes, bell peppers, and spinach; fold in half once set. Calories: 220. Protein: 15g (notably rich). Vitamin C: 18mg. A filling breakfast combining protein with vegetable vitamins and fiber.
recipe_veg_omelette.keywords: Omelette, vegetable omelette, egg recipe, breakfast

recipe_egg_fried_rice.title: Egg Fried Rice
recipe_egg_fried_rice.content: Recipe: Egg Fried Rice. Scramble eggs in a hot wok, then stir-fry with cooked rice, chopped vegetables (carrots, peas, spring onion), and soy sauce until well combined. Calories: 340. Protein: 12g. A quick, balanced one-pan meal combining protein, carbohydrates, and vegetables.
recipe_egg_fried_rice.keywords: Egg fried rice, egg recipe, rice dish

recipe_egg_curry.title: Egg Curry
recipe_egg_curry.content: Recipe: Egg Curry. Simmer hard-boiled eggs in a spiced onion-tomato gravy (ginger, garlic, turmeric, chili, garam masala) until the flavors soak in. Calories: 280. Protein: 14g (notably rich). A protein-rich Indian-style curry, usually served with rice or roti.
recipe_egg_curry.keywords: Egg curry, Indian egg recipe, curry

recipe_egg_bhurji.title: Egg Bhurji
recipe_egg_bhurji.content: Recipe: Egg Bhurji. An Indian-style spiced scrambled egg dish - whisked eggs cooked with sauteed onions, tomatoes, green chilies, and turmeric until fluffy and well combined. Calories: 210. Protein: 13g (notably rich). A quick, flavorful, high-protein breakfast or dinner dish, popular across India.
recipe_egg_bhurji.keywords: Egg bhurji, Indian egg recipe, scrambled egg, spiced eggs

recipe_egg_salad.title: Egg Salad
recipe_egg_salad.content: Recipe: Egg Salad. Mash hard-boiled eggs with a little mayonnaise or yogurt, mustard, and chopped celery/onion; season with salt and pepper. Calories: 250. Protein: 13g (notably rich). A protein-rich salad, great in a sandwich or on its own with greens; using yogurt instead of mayonnaise lightens it up.
recipe_egg_salad.keywords: Egg salad, egg recipe, protein salad

recipe_poached_eggs.title: Poached Eggs
recipe_poached_eggs.content: Recipe: Poached Eggs. Crack an egg into a small cup, then gently slide it into barely simmering water with a splash of vinegar; cook 3-4 minutes until the white sets but the yolk stays runny. Calories: 72 (per egg). Protein: 6g (notably rich). A classic, no-added-fat way to prepare eggs, often served over toast or greens (as in eggs Benedict-style dishes).

recipe_egg_sandwich.title: Egg Sandwich
recipe_egg_sandwich.content: Recipe: Egg Sandwich. Layer a fried or scrambled egg between whole-grain bread with lettuce, tomato, and a light spread of mayonnaise or avocado. Calories: 300. Protein: 16g (notably rich). Fiber: 4g. A balanced, portable breakfast or lunch combining protein, whole grains, and vegetables.
recipe_egg_sandwich.keywords: Egg sandwich, egg recipe, breakfast sandwich

recipe_vitb12_salmon_bowl.title: Vitamin B12 Salmon and Egg Power Bowl
recipe_vitb12_salmon_bowl.content: Recipe: Vitamin B12 Salmon and Egg Power Bowl. Grilled salmon, a soft-boiled egg, and sauteed spinach served over brown rice. Calories: 420. Protein: 32g (notably rich). Vitamin B12: 4.8mcg (over 150% of daily RDA). A vitamin-B12-rich bowl combining two of the best natural B12 sources (salmon and eggs), great for supporting nerve function and red blood cell production.
recipe_vitb12_salmon_bowl.keywords: Vitamin B12, salmon, egg, power bowl, B12 rich recipe

recipe_vitb6_banana_oat_smoothie.title: Vitamin B6 Banana Oat Smoothie
recipe_vitb6_banana_oat_smoothie.content: Recipe: Vitamin B6 Banana Oat Smoothie. Blend banana, rolled oats, milk, and a spoon of peanut butter until smooth. Calories: 310. Protein: 11g. Vitamin B6: 0.6mg (over 40% of daily RDA). A vitamin-B6-rich smoothie from banana and oats, supporting brain function and energy metabolism.
recipe_vitb6_banana_oat_smoothie.keywords: Vitamin B6, banana, oats, smoothie, B6 rich recipe

gen_nutrition.title: Nutrition
gen_nutrition.content: Nutrition is the science of how food and its components (nutrients) affect the health and functioning of the body. It involves macronutrients - carbohydrates, proteins, and fats - which provide energy and the building blocks for growth and repair, and micronutrients - vitamins and minerals - which support metabolic processes in smaller amounts. Good nutrition supports growth, immune function, energy levels, and long-term disease prevention, while poor nutrition can lead to deficiencies, obesity, and chronic disease. A balanced diet includes a variety of whole foods across all food groups in appropriate portions, tailored to individual needs like age, activity level, and health conditions.
gen_nutrition.keywords: Nutrition, macronutrients, micronutrients, balanced diet, food science

gen_vitamins_minerals.title: Vitamins and Minerals (Overview)
gen_vitamins_minerals.content: Vitamins and minerals are essential micronutrients the body needs in small amounts to function properly. Vitamins are organic compounds classified as fat-soluble (A, D, E, K - stored in body fat, absorbed alongside dietary fat) or water-soluble (the B-complex vitamins and vitamin C - not stored in the body, so they need regular intake). Minerals are inorganic elements, divided into macrominerals needed in larger amounts (calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, sodium, potassium) and trace minerals needed in tiny amounts (iron, zinc, iodine, selenium, copper). Together, vitamins and minerals support energy production, immune function, bone health, nerve signaling, and countless other body processes; a varied diet is usually the best way to get adequate amounts of each, though supplementation may be needed for diagnosed deficiencies.
gen_vitamins_minerals.keywords: Vitamins, minerals, micronutrients, fat soluble, water soluble, macrominerals, trace minerals

gen_immunology.title: Immunology
gen_immunology.content: Immunology is the branch of biology and medicine that studies the immune system - the body's defense network against infections, foreign substances, and abnormal cells. It covers innate immunity (the body's fast, general first-line defense, like skin and white blood cells) and adaptive immunity (a slower, highly specific defense that 'remembers' past infections, involving T-cells and B-cells). Immunology also studies how the immune system can malfunction, causing allergies, autoimmune diseases, or immunodeficiency (like in HIV/AIDS). Nutrition plays a significant supporting role in immunology, since many vitamins and minerals (vitamin C, vitamin D, zinc, and others) are directly required for proper immune cell development and function.
gen_immunology.keywords: Immunology, immune system, innate immunity, adaptive immunity, T-cells, B-cells
